narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 34, Year 3 school decile ranking of 2nd (well below the typical 5.5 average), Year 1 decile of 4th (also under the average 5.7), upper-middle family income, and plan to attend full-time—and predicted a 71.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high failure risk led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which signals weaker performance relative to peers in your school and strongly raised concerns about your bar readiness. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA, while modestly below the average of 3.21, actually worked slightly in your favor by suggesting some academic potential compared to lower performers. Your upper-middle family income also nudged the prediction toward failure, as the model associates it with patterns where students in similar brackets faced higher bar hurdles in the dataset. Overall, your metrics placed you below typical admitted students, especially in later-year grades and standardized scores, making the model view your bar success as riskier than average. While your full-time status aligns with most students (over 90%), it didn't offset the key drags from grades and income. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male applicant—many in similar demographics succeed with stronger academic signals—but a data-driven call based on these patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above the 36.7 average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your profile toward the passing side, opening doors at schools that value growth stories like yours. With targeted improvements, you can rewrite this prediction in your favor.
